findings.title: Brief claim contradicted by case file: Rivera has presented no evidence to rebut this presumption.
findings.description: The source file contains evidence of unsafe scaffolding conditions and evidence that a Harmon foreman was warned and dismissed the concern. That conflicts with the brief's assertion that Rivera presented no evidence to rebut reasonable-care or OSHA-compliance arguments.

evidence.quote: Rivera has presented no evidence to rebut this presumption.
The base plates on the failed section appeared to be resting on uneven ground, with one plate sitting on a loose plywood sheet rather than a stable footing.
Officers observed that portions of the scaffolding in the affected section showed visible rust and wear.
Some of the cross-braces on the lower tier appeared to have significant surface rust and looked like they had been reused from a previous job.
The base plate on the far-east end was sitting on a piece of plywood rather than on a proper mudsill or stable footing.
Donner told me, "We don't have time to re-do the base. It's been fine. Just get up there and get it done."

findings.confidence_reasoning: The record excerpts directly describe unsafe scaffold conditions and a warning to a Harmon foreman, which are plainly relevant to rebutting a due-care or OSHA-compliance presumption. The only uncertainty is procedural: “presented” could refer to admissible opposition evidence rather than the contents of the case file.

findings.title: Quotation attributed to Privette v. Superior Court appears doctored
findings.description: Privette is a real, well-known California Supreme Court case, but the quoted absolute language is not faithful to the doctrine. The word “never” materially overstates Privette: the case rejected peculiar-risk/vicarious liability in that setting, relying on workers’ compensation and delegation principles, but California law does not categorically immunize hirers from all liability for contractor-employee injuries arising from the work. Known actual language/rule: Privette held that the peculiar-risk doctrine does not impose vicarious liability on a hirer for injuries to an independent contractor's employees caused by the contractor's negligence in performing the contracted work. The doctrine is a general limitation/presumption, not an absolute “never liable” rule, and later California cases recognize exceptions such as retained control with affirmative contribution and concealed hazards.

evidence.quote: A hirer is never liable for injuries sustained by an independent contractor's employees when the injuries arise from the contracted work.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The challenged quote uses absolute “never liable” language that conflicts with the canonical Privette framework and recognized exceptions. Confidence is capped because the supplied evidence does not include the actual Privette text, but the doctrine is well established.

findings.title: Authority does not support the stated absolute Privette proposition
findings.description: The quoted absolute statement that a hirer is “never liable” materially overstates Privette. California law recognizes a Privette presumption of nonliability, not an absolute immunity; later cases recognize exceptions such as retained control with affirmative contribution, concealed hazards, and negligent provision of unsafe equipment.

evidence.quote: Id. at 702.

findings.confidence_reasoning: This is the same Privette overstatement addressed more completely in finding-12; the legal point is strongly supported by the well-known Privette doctrine and its exceptions. Confidence is slightly reduced because this finding's own evidence only shows the short-form citation, not the full challenged sentence.

findings.title: Cited authority could not be located: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc.
findings.description: I do not recognize Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc. The quoted OSHA-compliance presumption is suspiciously perfect for the brief’s argument, and 887 F.2d appears chronologically inconsistent with a 1991 Ninth Circuit citation based on general reporter chronology. Jurisdiction concern: Even if real, a Ninth Circuit decision would not bind a California Superior Court on California negligence law, though it could be persuasive on federal issues.

evidence.quote: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc., 887 F.2d 1204, 1209 (9th Cir. 1991) ("Where an employer demonstrates full compliance with applicable OSHA standards, it is entitled to a rebuttable presumption that it met the standard of care in negligence.").

findings.confidence_reasoning: The reporter-year mismatch for 887 F.2d and the unusually tailored OSHA presumption are concrete warning signs, but the evidence does not include a definitive citator or docket search. Confidence is therefore moderate rather than near-certain.

findings.title: Cited authority could not be located: Whitmore v. Delgado Scaffolding Co.
findings.description: I do not recognize this C.D. Cal. case, and the case name and parenthetical are suspiciously tailored to the brief’s exact scaffolding facts and Privette argument. The asserted holding also reads like a made-for-the-brief summary rather than a familiar reported federal decision. Jurisdiction concern: Even if real, a C.D. Cal. federal district decision would not bind a California Superior Court on California tort law; it would at most be persuasive.

evidence.quote: Whitmore v. Delgado Scaffolding Co., 334 F. Supp. 2d 1189, 1195 (C.D. Cal. 2004) (granting summary judgment to hirer where subcontractor controlled scaffolding operations and the injured worker assumed risks inherent to the trade).

findings.confidence_reasoning: The citation and parenthetical are suspicious, but the evidence supplied does not include a database search result or other mechanical proof that the case does not exist. The finding is kept because fabricated authority would be serious if confirmed, but the current support is largely nonrecognition and contextual suspicion.

findings.description: The location is corroborated, but the brief's date is not. All source documents place the incident on March 12, 2021, which conflicts with the brief's March 14, 2021 date.

findings.title: Time-barred heading contradicted by concession that filing falls within limitations window
findings.description: The section heading asserts the claims are time-barred, but the body concedes the filing falls within this window under the statute of limitations (CCP 335.1 / two years = 730 days). Filing on 2023-03-10 is 726 days after the incident on 2021-03-14, which is inside the 730-day window. The time-barred framing is therefore self-defeating.

evidence.quote: D. Rivera's Claims Are Time-Barred

While Rivera's filing falls nominally within this window, Harmon reserves the right to challenge the accrual date based on Rivera's own knowledge of his injuries, which the medical records indicate were immediately apparent.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The internal contradiction is clear: the heading says the claims are time-barred while the body says the filing falls within the limitations window. Confidence is not higher because the filing date itself is separately flagged as unsupported, and the limitations calculation is not fully documented in the supplied evidence.

findings.title: Authority does not support the stated proposition: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc.
findings.description: Seabright is not principally a case holding that compliance with statutory safety requirements is highly probative of due care. Its actual holding favors delegation/nonliability under Privette even for regulatory safety duties. The brief’s broader statement that OSHA compliance “effectively insulat[es]” the hirer from tort liability also overstates California law, because Privette has exceptions and regulatory compliance is not generally absolute immunity.

evidence.quote: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc., 52 Cal.4th 590, 598 (2011).

findings.confidence_reasoning: Seabright's general holding is a known California Privette-delegation decision, so the claimed OSHA-compliance insulation is materially overstated. Confidence is limited because this finding's evidence quotes only the citation rather than the full sentence allegedly characterizing Seabright.

findings.title: Quotation attributed to Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc. appears fabricated
findings.description: I do not recognize this case as a real Ninth Circuit authority, and the citation is suspect: 887 F.2d corresponds to the late 1980s, not 1991. More importantly, the quoted OSHA-compliance rule is not a known federal negligence rule; OSHA generally does not enlarge or diminish common-law duties, and compliance is at most evidence of due care in many contexts, not an entitlement to a rebuttable presumption. The quote reads like a manufactured rule tailored to the brief’s argument.

judicial_memo: The brief contains several significant defects, including record contradictions and overstated or unverified legal authority. Most seriously, Section III.B asserts that Rivera presented no evidence to rebut reasonable-care or OSHA-compliance arguments, but the source file contains evidence of unsafe scaffolding conditions and that a Harmon foreman was warned and dismissed the concern. Section III.A attributes to Privette an absolute “never liable” rule; Privette supports a limitation on vicarious peculiar-risk liability, not categorical immunity, and later California law recognizes exceptions including retained control with affirmative contribution, concealed hazards, and negligent provision of unsafe equipment. Two cited authorities—Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc. and Whitmore v. Delgado Scaffolding Co.—could not be located in available materials, with the stated confidence lower as to Whitmore. The brief also repeatedly states the incident date as March 14, 2021, while all source documents identify March 12, 2021, and includes several unsupported factual assertions.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: I recognize Privette v. Superior Court, 5 Cal.4th 689 (1993), as a leading California Supreme Court decision; the reporter, volume, court, and year are consistent.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Privette rejected imposing peculiar-risk liability on a hirer for injuries to an independent contractor’s employee covered by workers’ compensation. It is the foundation of the California Privette doctrine, under which a hirer is generally presumed not liable for such injuries, subject to recognized exceptions.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: The asserted proposition is framed as a presumption/general rule, which Privette and its progeny support. The brief should not omit that later California Supreme Court cases recognize exceptions, including retained control affirmatively contributing to injury (Hooker), concealed hazards known to the hirer (Kinsman), and negligent furnishing of unsafe equipment (McKown).

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: This is an id. citation to the real Privette decision identified in cite-1. I recognize the underlying case, though I cannot confirm the exact wording at page 702 from memory.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Privette generally bars peculiar-risk liability against a hirer for injuries to an independent contractor’s employee, because workers’ compensation is the employee’s remedy against the contractor and the hirer has delegated worksite safety responsibility to the contractor.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: The quoted absolute statement that a hirer is “never liable” materially overstates Privette. California law recognizes a Privette presumption of nonliability, not an absolute immunity; later cases recognize exceptions such as retained control with affirmative contribution, concealed hazards, and negligent provision of unsafe equipment.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: I do not recognize this C.D. Cal. case, and the case name and parenthetical are suspiciously tailored to the brief’s exact scaffolding facts and Privette argument. The asserted holding also reads like a made-for-the-brief summary rather than a familiar reported federal decision.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: Because I cannot verify the case and see concrete fabrication signals, I cannot assess whether it supports the asserted proposition.
citation_verdicts.jurisdiction_concern: Even if real, a C.D. Cal. federal district decision would not bind a California Superior Court on California tort law; it would at most be persuasive.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: I do not recognize Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc. The quoted OSHA-compliance presumption is suspiciously perfect for the brief’s argument, and 887 F.2d appears chronologically inconsistent with a 1991 Ninth Circuit citation based on general reporter chronology.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: Because the authority appears likely fabricated, I cannot verify support. Substantively, the asserted rule is also suspect: compliance with safety regulations may be evidence of due care, but I do not know a California rule creating a rebuttable presumption of reasonable care merely from OSHA compliance.
citation_verdicts.jurisdiction_concern: Even if real, a Ninth Circuit decision would not bind a California Superior Court on California negligence law, though it could be persuasive on federal issues.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: I recognize Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc., 52 Cal.4th 590 (2011), as a California Supreme Court Privette-doctrine case; the citation details are consistent.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Seabright held that a hirer generally delegates to an independent contractor any tort duty the hirer owes the contractor’s employees to ensure the safety of the worksite, including duties arising from Cal-OSHA-type safety regulations. The hirer therefore was not liable to the contractor’s employee on the theory that it failed to comply with such safety requirements.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: Seabright is not principally a case holding that compliance with statutory safety requirements is highly probative of due care. Its actual holding favors delegation/nonliability under Privette even for regulatory safety duties. The brief’s broader statement that OSHA compliance “effectively insulat[es]” the hirer from tort liability also overstates California law, because Privette has exceptions and regulatory compliance is not generally absolute immunity.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: California Code of Civil Procedure section 335.1 is a well-known California statute setting the limitations period for personal injury and wrongful-death-type claims.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Section 335.1 provides a two-year limitations period for actions for assault, battery, injury to, or death of an individual caused by the wrongful act or neglect of another.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: The asserted proposition that California personal-injury claims generally have a two-year statute of limitations is supported. However, as the brief itself notes, a March 10, 2023 filing for a March 14, 2021 injury appears to fall within two years, so the surrounding time-bar argument is not supported by this statute on the stated dates.
